Decentralized brains.<[silence:700ms]> Parallel problem-solving.<[silence:700ms]>

The term eight-legged problem-solving refers to how octopuses—with two-thirds of their neurons in their arms—think and act without a central command.<[silence:700ms]> Unlike humans, whose brain directs every move, an octopus arm can taste, smell, and react on its own.<[silence:700ms]> It’s like eight mini-brains collaborating, not waiting for orders.<[silence:700ms]>

Here’s how it works:<[silence:120ms]> Each arm is packed with sensors and motor neurons, letting it explore a coral reef while the main brain focuses elsewhere.<[silence:700ms]> If one arm grabs a crab, the others adjust instantly—no hesitation.<[silence:700ms]> Scientists tested this by cutting the nerve link between an arm and its brain;<[silence:100ms]> the arm still dodged threats and grabbed food.<[silence:700ms]> Even severed arms "walk" away and react to stimuli for hours.<[silence:700ms]>

This isn’t chaos.<[silence:700ms]> It’s efficiency.<[silence:700ms]> A 2015 study showed octopuses solving mazes faster when using multiple arms at once, as if each limb votes on the best path.<[silence:700ms]> No other animal does this.<[silence:700ms]>

Three related subjects are distributed intelligence, neural autonomy in robots, and how squid communicate visually.<[silence:700ms]>
clean_story:
Decentralized brains. Parallel problem-solving.

The term eight-legged problem-solving refers to how octopuses—with two-thirds of their neurons in their arms—think and act without a central command. Unlike humans, whose brain directs every move, an octopus arm can taste, smell, and react on its own. It’s like eight mini-brains collaborating, not waiting for orders.

Here’s how it works: Each arm is packed with sensors and motor neurons, letting it explore a coral reef while the main brain focuses elsewhere. If one arm grabs a crab, the others adjust instantly—no hesitation. Scientists tested this by cutting the nerve link between an arm and its brain; the arm still dodged threats and grabbed food. Even severed arms "walk" away and react to stimuli for hours.

This isn’t chaos. It’s efficiency. A 2015 study showed octopuses solving mazes faster when using multiple arms at once, as if each limb votes on the best path. No other animal does this.

Three related subjects are distributed intelligence, neural autonomy in robots, and how squid communicate visually.
